elements.sent-2411-001: To improve persistence diagram representation learning, we propose Multiset Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is the first neural network that utilizes attention mechanisms specifically designed for multisets as inputs and offers rigorous theoretical guarantees of permutation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-003: The architecture integrates multiset-enhanced attentions with a pool-decomposition scheme, allowing multiplicities to be preserved across equivariant layers.
elements.sent-2411-004: This capability enables full leverage of multiplicities while significantly reducing both computational and spatial complexity compared to the Set Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, our method can greatly benefit from clustering as a preprocessing step to further minimize complexity, an advantage not possessed by the Set Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experimental results demonstrate that the Multiset Transformer outperforms existing neural network methods in the realm of persistence diagram representation learning \footnote{The source code for Multiset Transformer can be accessed at: \href{https://github.com/minghuax/MST {https://github.com/minghuax/MST .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: In recent years, the field of machine learning has seen the growing importance of multisets, often referred to as bags.
elements.sent-2411-008: These multisets provide an advanced form of traditional sets by allowing for multiple instances of identical elements.
elements.sent-2411-009: Descriptors based on histograms and the bag-of-words model, both examples of multisets, are not only common in Multiple Instance Learning \citep{dietterich1997solving, babenko2010robust, quellec2017multiple , but also in natural language processing and text mining.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Furthermore, they are recognized as among the primary representation techniques for tasks such as object categorization, as well as image and video recognition \citep{dalal2005histograms, zhang2010understanding, welleck2018loss .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This widespread use highlights the increasing importance and versatility of multisets in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-012: Given their prevalence and critical role, understanding and mastering representation for multisets becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the realm of topological data analysis (TDA), persistence diagrams (PDs) stand out as a pivotal descriptor for persistent homology (PH), offering a multi-scale depiction of a space's intrinsic topological attributes \citep{edelsbrunner2002topological .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Such topological insights have proven instrumental in deciphering complex biological structures, such as neural connections \citep{giusti2016two , and have found applications in material science for analyzing porosity and nanomaterial structures \citep{nakamura2015persistent .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The recent fusion of PH-derived features with machine learning has enhanced both model performance and interpretability \citep{hofer2017deep,horn2021topological .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Nevertheless, the intrinsic multiset characteristics of PDs, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:pds , present significant challenges for their direct integration into traditional machine learning models.}
elements.sent-2411-017: These models predominantly necessitate inputs in a vector format, thus complicating the utilization of PDs.
elements.sent-2411-018: This essential transformation of PDs into vectors is known as vectorization \citep{ali2023survey , which align with our MST architecture.}

elements.sent-2411-019: Our contributions in this paper can be outlined in two primary areas: the introduction of a novel Multiset Transformer (MST) and its subsequent application in PD representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-020: The MST architecture is uniquely tailored to accept multisets as input, leveraging multiplicities to allocate greater attention to items with higher frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-021: This innovative design allows MST to utilize the multiplicities in multiset inputs while significantly reducing spatial and computational complexity compared to the Set Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-022: When applied to PD representation learning, MST consistently outperforms the existing methodologies across a majority of the datasets we evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, our experimental results indicate that MST inherently offers an approximation by clustering the multiset prior to processing.
elements.sent-2411-024: The transformer model, as introduced by \citet{vaswani2017attention , has revolutionized the field of deep learning, particularly within natural language processing (NLP).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Its inception has spurred the development of numerous variants tailored to various data structures and applications, as outlined in the extensive surveys by \citet{lin2022survey and \citet{khan2022transformers .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: A prominent example is the Set Transformer \citep{lee2019set , which offers a novel approach to processing unordered sets.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, to the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first transformer variant specifically designed for handling multisets.
elements.sent-2411-028: Persistent homology and its PD vectorization have played a pivotal role in the integration of TDA into machine learning, as evidenced by works such as \citet{dey2022computational and \citet{ali2023survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Traditional vectorization methods, including persistence landscapes \citep{bubenik2015statistical and persistence images \citep{adams2017persistence , have garnered significant attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The comprehensive survey by \citet{ali2023survey offers a structured framework, clarifying the various vectorization techniques available.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In addition to these foundational methods, there has been a growing interest in adapting machine learning architectures to this domain.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, \citet{hofer2017deep introduced an innovative input layer for deep neural networks that processes topological signatures, computing a learnable parameterized projection.}
elements.sent-2411-033: This idea was further refined by the same authors, who developed a neural network layer adept at handling barcodes by projecting points using parameterized functionals, as detailed in \citet{hofer2019learning .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Another notable contribution is PersLay, a specialized neural network layer for processing PDs, proposed by \citet{carriere2020perslay .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Persformer architecture, introduced by \citet{reinauer2021persformer , exemplifies the application of transformers to PD vectorization, presenting a transformer-centric methodology for PDs.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Notably, our contribution diverges from the aforementioned works in a distinctive manner.
elements.sent-2411-037: The MST uniquely treats PDs as multisets rather than viewing them as lists of points that may be duplicated.
elements.sent-2411-038: This approach enables the model to incorporate a clustering phase before vectorization, effectively tackling the inherent computational challenges within this domain.
elements.sent-2411-039: A multiset can be viewed as an extension of the conventional set, permitting the inclusion of multiple occurrences of identical elements.
elements.sent-2411-040: Formally, a multiset is defined by a base set \(X\) accompanied by its multiplicity map \(M: X\rightarrow \mathbb Z^+\).
elements.sent-2411-042: Here, the base set \(X\) is represented as \(\{a,b\}\), and the associated multiplicity map \(M\) is such that \(M(a)=1\) and \(M(b)=2\).
elements.sent-2411-043: Inherent to their definitions, both sets and multisets are indifferent to the order of their elements.
elements.sent-2411-044: Consequently, functions defined on sets or multisets inherently possess the property of permutation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-045: Formally, given any permutation \(\sigma\) of the set of indices \(\{1,2, \ldots, n\}\) corresponding to a vector \(\mathbf x\), \(f\) is permutation invariant if and only if: \begin{equation}f\left(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\right) = f\left(x_{\sigma(1)}, x_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, x_{\sigma(n)}\right),\end{equation} for all such permutations \(\sigma\).
elements.sent-2411-046: Conversely, while the concept of permutation invariance revolves around the indifference to order, there exists a contrasting property where the order is preserved, termed as permutation equivariance.
elements.sent-2411-047: Formally, for every permutation \(\sigma\) of the set of indices \(\{1,2, \ldots, n\}\): \begin{equation}f\left(x_{\sigma(1)}, \ldots, x_{\sigma(n)}\right) = \left(f_{\sigma(1)}(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_{\sigma(n)}(\mathbf{x})\right),\end{equation} for all such permutations \(\sigma\).
elements.sent-2411-048: While \citet{zhang2021multiset introduced the above permutation equivariance as set equivariance and further proposed multiset-equivariance as its relaxation, our architecture distinctly separates the base set from its multiplicities.}
elements.sent-2411-049: This separation results in set like inputs, making set equivariance the appropriate property for our approach.
elements.sent-2411-050: To achieve permutation invariance, the pool-decomposition scheme is commonly employed.
elements.sent-2411-051: Given a set \( X = \{ x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \} \in \mathcal X\), we define transformation functions \( \phi: \mathcal X \rightarrow \mathbb R^h \) and \( \rho: \mathbb R^h \rightarrow \mathbb R^d \), where \( h \) and \( d \) denote the hidden and output dimensions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-052: The operator \( \operatorname{pool \) signifies a permutation-invariant pooling operation, such as sum, average, or max.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, a function \( f \) on \( X \) is expressed as: \begin{equation}f(X) = \rho\left(\operatorname{pool}_{i=1}^n \phi\left(x_i\right)\right) \label{eq:pool-decomposition}\end{equation} This approach is referenced in multiple studies, including \citet{ravanbakhsh2016deep, qi2017pointnet, zaheer2017deep, carriere2020perslay, reinauer2021persformer .}
elements.sent-2411-054: In our work, we adopt the pool-decomposition schema as a cornerstone for our MST design.
elements.sent-2411-055: From a machine learning perspective, the problem can be formally described as follows: Let \(\mathcal{D}\) denote a bounded, finite multiset space representing the data space, let \(\mathcal{H}\) denote a Hilbert space serving as the feature space, and let \(\mathcal{Y}\) represent the target space.
elements.sent-2411-056: Consider a learning system characterized by the mappings \begin{equation}D \xrightarrow{f_{\theta}} H \xrightarrow{g_{\phi}} Y,\end{equation} where the function \(f_{\theta}\), parameterized by \(\theta\), maps elements of \(\mathcal{D}\) to representations in \(\mathcal{H}\), and the function \(g_{\phi}\), parameterized by \(\phi\), predicts outcomes in \(\mathcal{Y}\) based on these representations.
elements.sent-2411-057: The objective is to develop and refine the representation \(f_{\theta}\) such that \(g_{\phi}\) can achieve better predictions by minimizing the loss function \(L(g_{\phi (f_{\theta (x)), y)\), where \((x, y) \in \mathcal{D \times \mathcal{Y}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: To illustrate, in this paper, we employ MST as the model for the representation function \(f_\theta\).
elements.sent-2411-059: From an application perspective, the problem centers on acquiring representations of PDs suitable for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-060: These PDs are derived from graphs, which are fundamentally multisets, as depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:pds .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Inherently, PDs do not possess intrinsic bounds or finite limits; yet, for practical purposes, they are typically preprocessed into bounded, finite multisets.
elements.sent-2411-062: For the purposes of this discussion, it is presupposed that PDs are treated as bounded and finite multisets.
elements.sent-2411-063: This representation learning framework adheres to key criteria outlined subsequently:.
elements.sent-2411-064: Permutation Invariance: Given the fundamental properties of multisets, the representation must be order-agnostic.
elements.sent-2411-065: This ensures a consistent representation, irrespective of the ordering of elements.
elements.sent-2411-066: Practical Feasibility: The training process for representations should be computationally feasible, emphasizing feasibility and scalability, particularly for large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-067: Feature Retention: It is imperative for the representation to capture and retain key features.
elements.sent-2411-068: This enables subsequent machine learning tasks to effectively exploit the data's underlying structure.
elements.sent-2411-069: By addressing these priorities, we aim to bridge the gap between multiset representation learning and its applications ensuring that the complex structure of the data remains most informative throughout the representation process.
elements.sent-2411-070: The Multiset Transformer (MST) is based on the fundamental idea of leveraging the multiplicity inherent in multisets to influence the attention score function.
elements.sent-2411-071: Essentially, the objective is to steer the model's attention towards items with relatively higher multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-072: This is grounded in the belief that items with higher multiplicities may carry more importance or relevance in certain contexts, and thus should be given more weight during the attention process.
elements.sent-2411-073: To provide a comprehensive explanation of the MST, it is imperative to first explain the underlying mechanisms of attention.
elements.sent-2411-074: The attention mechanism we employ is consistent with the one presented by \citet{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Given \(n\) query vectors \( Q \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times d_q \), \(m\) key vectors \( K \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times d_k \), and \(m\) value vectors \( V \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times d_v \), where \(d_q\), \(d_k\), and \(d_v\) represent their respective dimensions, the attention function \(\operatorname{Att (Q, K, V)\) is designed to map queries \( Q \) to outputs using the key-value pairs.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: This is mathematically represented as: \begin{equation}\operatorname{Att}(Q, K, V ; \omega)=\omega(Q K^{\top}) V .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: The pairwise dot product \( Q K^{\top \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times m \) serves as a measure of similarity between each pair of query and key vectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The weights for this dot product are computed using the activation function \(\omega\).
elements.sent-2411-079: Consequently, the output \(\omega(Q K^{\top ) V\) is essentially a weighted sum of \( V \).}
elements.sent-2411-080: Here, a value vector from \( V \) receives a higher weight if its associated key vector has a larger dot product with the query.
elements.sent-2411-081: Note that the dot product \( QK^\top \) necessitates \( d_q = d_k \) for dimensional consistency.
elements.sent-2411-082: Building upon this, we specifically utilize the scaled dot-product attention in our construction, which is given by: \begin{equation}\operatorname{Att}(Q, K, V)=\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt d_k}\right) V . \label{eq:att}\end{equation} This formulation ensures that the attention weights are appropriately normalized, and the scaling factor \(\sqrt d_k\) aids in stabilizing the magnitudes of the dot products, especially when \(d_k\) is large.
elements.sent-2411-083: Furthermore, our architecture incorporates multihead attention, a technique that facilitates capturing a diverse range of relationships and dependencies within the input data.
elements.sent-2411-084: Each attention head operates independently, allowing the model to focus on different aspects of the input simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-085: Additional details on multihead attention can be found in the Appendix for clarity.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Multiset Attention Mechanisms in the Transformer
elements.sent-2411-086: Within the framework of the MST, attention is employed in two distinct manners: self-attention and attention with learnable queries.
elements.sent-2411-087: To clarify these mechanisms, we first establish the necessary notations and their interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-088: A multiset is denoted as \((X, M_X)\), where \(X\), the base set, is represented as an array of points in \(\mathbb{R ^{n \times d}\).}
elements.sent-2411-089: The multiplicities associated with these points are specified by \(M_X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times 1}\), where each element of \(M_X\) corresponds to the multiplicity of the respective element in \(X\).}
elements.sent-2411-090: It is important to note that a multiset reduces to a conventional set when all elements of \(M_X\) are equal to unity, that is, \(M_i = 1\) for each \(i\).
elements.sent-2411-091: In this section, we introduce the concept of multiset attention, a novel approach designed to handle input multisets and their associated multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-092: The primary motivation behind this mechanism is to incorporate the multiplicity information into the traditional attention mechanism, thereby enhancing its ability to focus on elements with larger multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-093: Given input base sets \( Q \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times d \) and \( X \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times d \), along with their respective multiplicities \( M_Q \in \mathbb R^{n\times 1 \) and \( M_X\in \mathbb R^{m\times 1 \), we define the multiset-enhanced attention weights as: \begin{equation}A(Q, X) : = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha B \right) X, B : = \frac{(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F + \varepsilon}. \label{eq:att_bias}\end{equation} Here, \( \|\|_F \) denotes the Frobenius norm, which is utilized to normalize this new term.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The parameter \( \alpha \) is learnable, allowing the model to adjust its influence during training.
elements.sent-2411-095: Intuitively, the introduction of the multiplicities term~(\ref{eq:att_bias ) enables the attention mechanism to allocate greater emphasis to elements with higher multiplicities.}
elements.sent-2411-096: This enhancement augments the mechanism's ability to selectively focus on the most salient elements within a multiset.
elements.sent-2411-097: Self-attention, as described by \citet{vaswani2017attention , is characterized by the attention scores being derived directly from the input.}
elements.sent-2411-098: This mechanism inherently captures the intra-correlation present within the input data.
elements.sent-2411-099: Building upon the multiset attention framework discussed in the previous sections, we extend this concept to introduce the multiset self-attention.
elements.sent-2411-100: Given an input matrix \( X \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times d \) with multiplicity \( M_X \in \mathbb R^{m\times 1 \), multiset self-attention is achieved by setting \( Q = K = V = X \), leading to the self-attention \( A(X,X) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: This self-attention \( A(X,X) \) is notable for its property of permutation equivariance, which we formalize in the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-102: The multiset self-attention, represented as \( A(X, X) \), is permutation equivariant.
elements.sent-2411-103: In the previous section, we introduce a permutation equivariant multiset self-attention.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, to ensure the permutation invariant property in a MST, it is necessary to incorporate a permutation invariant operator, i.e., the \(\operatorname{pool}\) operator in Equation~(\ref{eq:pool-decomposition ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: The core of our approach lies in the multiset attention with learnable queries.
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, we set \( K = V = X \) and introduce a learnable matrix \( Q \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times d \), where \( n \) is a user-defined parameter.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: It is worth noting that the query is independent of the input and is shared across all input instances, enabling the capture of common features during training.
elements.sent-2411-108: To account for multiplicity, we define the multiset attention with learnable queries as: \begin{equation}A_Q(X) := \left( \operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + B \right) X, B := \frac{M_{\alpha}(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F +\varepsilon}. \label{eq:att_q_bias}\end{equation} where \( M_{\alpha \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times 1 \) are learnable parameters.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Similarly, we formalize its permutation invariant in the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-110: The multiset attention with learnable queries \(A_Q(X)\) is permutation invariant.
elements.sent-2411-111: The terms introduced in Equations~(\ref{eq:att_bias ) and~(\ref{eq:att_q_bias ) are not the only possible choices, yet they are carefully selected for their alignment with several key aspects of our framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Firstly, these multiplicity bias terms successfully incorporate biases associated with multiplicities into the attention weights, aligning with our philosophy of assigning attention based on multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-113: Moreover, they are key in maintaining the permutation equivariant or invariant properties, which are important for our model's integrity.
elements.sent-2411-114: Finally, they demonstrate mathematical consistency by reducing to zero for set inputs, when \( M_Q = \mathbf{1 \) and \( M_X = \mathbf{1 \), these attentions degenerate to the original ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Multiset self-attention and multiset attention with learnable queries are crucial in the construction of the MST.
elements.sent-2411-116: To ensure clarity and maintain consistency with existing literature, we adopt and slightly modify terminologies introduced by \citet{lee2019set .}
elements.sent-2411-117: The cornerstone of our proposed architecture is the Multiset Attention Block (MAB).
elements.sent-2411-118: This block can be described using the following formulation: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MAB}(Q, X) := \operatorname{LN}(H + \operatorname{FFN}(H)), H := \operatorname{LN}(Q + A(Q,X)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: Here, \(\operatorname{FFN}\) denotes a position-wise feedforward layer, specifically row-wise.
elements.sent-2411-120: The symbol \(\operatorname{LN}\) represents layer normalization, as illustrated by \citet{ba2016layer .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Based on the MAB, we construct several foundational components important for the MST.
elements.sent-2411-122: The multiset attention block with learnable queries, denoted as \( \operatorname{MAB _Q \), is formulated for a given set \( X \).}
elements.sent-2411-123: Mathematically, it can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MAB}_Q(X) := \operatorname{LN}(H + \operatorname{FFN}(H)), H := \operatorname{LN}(Q + A_Q(X)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-124: The Multiset Self-Attention Block (SAB) is a specialized case of the MAB wherein the input set serves as both the key and value.
elements.sent-2411-125: This block can be represented as: \begin{equation}\operatorname{SAB}(X) := \operatorname{MAB}(X, X).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-126: Inspired by the Induced Set Attention Block (ISAB) as presented in \citet{lee2019set , we also introduce the Induced Multiset Attention Block (IMAB).}
elements.sent-2411-127: This block is designed to provide similar functionality but with reduced computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-129: It is evident that both \( \operatorname{SAB \) and \( \operatorname{IMAB \) maintain permutation equivariance.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The MST is formulated on the foundation of a pool-decomposition scheme, as articulated in Equation~(\ref{eq:pool-decomposition ).}
elements.sent-2411-132: The rationale for this architectural decision is bifurcated.
elements.sent-2411-133: Initially, it guarantees that the model attains an optimal level of expressiveness, facilitating the capture of complex patterns and interrelationships within the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-134: Concomitantly, it is imperative to note that the model embodies a permutation-invariant property, a requisite dictated by the inherent characteristics of a multiset.
elements.sent-2411-135: The overall architecture of the MST, detailing its various components and their interconnections, is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:mt .}
elements.sent-2411-136: Within the given figure, a multiset is denoted as \( (X, M_X) \), where \( X \) represents the base set, and \( M_X \) indicates its corresponding multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-137: The architecture consists of two main components: the Equivariant Layers and an Invariant Layer.
elements.sent-2411-138: The Equivariant Layers are structured as a sequence of permutation equivariance blocks, which can be either SABs or IMABs.
elements.sent-2411-139: The outputs from these layers, denoted as \( H \), possess permutation equivariance.
elements.sent-2411-140: Given that \( H \) maintains permutation equivariance in relation to \( X \), it follows that the multiplicities \( M_X \) are intrinsically the multiplicities of \( H \).
elements.sent-2411-141: This architectural design ensures that the multiplicities not only enrich the Equivariant Layers but also strengthen the subsequent Invariant Layer.
elements.sent-2411-142: The Invariant Layer can be constructed either by employing invariant operators or by applying the \( \operatorname{MAB _Q \) operation.}
elements.sent-2411-143: This procedure ultimately results in the final output \( R \), serving as the representation of the multiset.
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore, a conventional architecture wherein the Equivariant Layers are constructed via IMAB and the Invariant Layer is performed by \( \operatorname{MAB _Q \) can be expressed as: \begin{equation}H = \operatorname{IMAB} (\operatorname{IMAB} ( \ldots \operatorname{IMAB}(X)\ldots )), R = \operatorname{MAB}_Q (H).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-145: This architecture ensures a balance between expressiveness and complexity, making it suitable for a wide range of applications.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Complexity Analysis of Multiset Transformer and Set Transformer
elements.sent-2411-146: To thoroughly highlight the advantages of the MST, we conduct a complexity analysis comparing it to the Set Transformer (ST), particularly focusing on scenarios involving multiset inputs.
elements.sent-2411-147: Notably, the attention mechanism significantly influences the complexity of these architectures, forming the core of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-148: For a multiset \(X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times d}\), where \(d\) is fixed, and accompanied by its multiplicity information \(M_X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times 1}\), it's important to acknowledge that the ST processes inputs in the form of lists.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: In cases where \(M_X\) results in a multiset list, the size of the list can scale to \(\mathcal{O (nm)\), with \(m\) representing the maximum multiplicity within \(M_X\).}
elements.sent-2411-150: Consequently, the complexities of the Set Attention Block (SAB) and Induced Set Attention Block (ISAB) operations in the Set Transformer are \(\mathcal{O (n^2m^2)\) and \(\mathcal{O (nmq)\), respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Here, \(q\) denotes the number of inducing points in ISAB.
elements.sent-2411-152: In contrast, the MST, designed to handle multiplicities without duplicating elements, demonstrates complexities of \(\mathcal{O (n^2)\) and \(\mathcal{O (nq)\) for the SAB and IMAB, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: This comparison underscores the inherent lower complexity of the MST when processing multiset inputs.
elements.sent-2411-154: Similarly, the space complexity exhibits the same favorable results, with \(d\) being held constant.
elements.sent-2411-155: In the experiments section, our studies are divided into two primary parts: synthetic experiments and persistence diagram (PD) representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-156: The goal of the synthetic experiments is to provide a preliminary study to confirm that the framework operates as expected.
elements.sent-2411-157: On the other hand, the PD representation learning aims to demonstrate the effectiveness and relevance of our framework on real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-158: Through these experiments, we attempt to substantiate both the theoretical foundations and the practical usefulness of our proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-159: In the synthetic experiments, our goal was to demonstrate the ability of the MST to highlight elements that appear with the highest frequency within a multiset.
elements.sent-2411-160: We employed synthetic samples composed of multisets of 2-dimensional points.
elements.sent-2411-161: The true label is determined based on the element that appears with the highest frequency.
elements.sent-2411-162: For the purpose of learning representations of these multisets, we employed the MST, both with and without multiplicity inputs.

elements.sent-2411-201: In our results, we primarily showcase two variations of the model: the MST applied directly and the MST preceded by a clustering preprocess.
elements.sent-2411-202: Detailed comparisons and visualizations of the MST model with the clustering preprocess can be found in Figure~\ref{fig:mt-approx .}
elements.sent-2411-203: In our experiments, we utilize the DBSCAN clustering algorithm, with the hyperparameters detailed in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our primary goal was to assess the effectiveness of the MST in the context of PD representation learning, and the results yielded valuable insights on multiple fronts.
elements.sent-2411-205: Firstly, the results demonstrate that, across a majority of datasets, the performance of the MST without clustering preprocessing exceeds that of PERSLAY.
elements.sent-2411-206: This trend holds true for both Extended and Ordinary PDs, thereby highlighting the robustness and superiority of the MST in the task of PD vectorization using neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-207: In comparing the performance of the MST with and without clustering, it may initially be hypothesized that the incorporation of clustering could detrimentally impact model performance due to data simplification.
elements.sent-2411-208: Contrary to this intuition, our empirical results reveal that the integration of clustering with the MST generally results in only a marginal decrease in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-209: This is particularly notable in the case of Extended PDs for the COLLAB dataset, where even when reduced to just \(0.01\) of the original input size, the performance still surpasses that of the baseline model.
elements.sent-2411-210: Given the substantial benefits in terms of computational complexity reduction and improved scalability, this slight trade-off in accuracy is deemed justifiable.
elements.sent-2411-211: More interestingly, in specific scenarios such as Extended PDs for the MUTAG and Ordinary PDs for DHFR and COLLAB, clustering not only mitigates the anticipated performance degradation but also actively boosts accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-212: This nuanced observation suggests that clustering might capture intrinsic data structures beneficial for representation in certain contexts.
elements.sent-2411-213: In this section, we conduct an ablation study to examine the entries presented in Table~\ref{tab:results .}
elements.sent-2411-214: The primary aim of this analysis is to demonstrate the performance improvements attributable to the introduction of multiplicity terms.
elements.sent-2411-215: To thoroughly investigate the influence of multiplicities on our model, we specifically focus on entries characterized by lower data ratios in Table~\ref{tab:results .}

elements.sent-2411-217: Consistently across all datasets, the MST model, when fully equipped with PD and its associated multiplicities, demonstrates superior performance over its counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-218: A direct comparison between the complete MST model and its variant, MST (w/o mult.), provides insights into the pivotal role of the multiplicities terms within the MST framework.
elements.sent-2411-219: Notably, the exclusion of these terms from the model's architecture invariably leads to a decrease in accuracy for most datasets.
elements.sent-2411-220: To illustrate, datasets such as MUTAG, NCI1, NCI109, PROTEIN, and IMDB-B exhibit an accuracy reduction exceeding \(1\%\).
elements.sent-2411-221: As anticipated, the omission of the entire PD results in a more pronounced decline in accuracy than merely excluding its multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-222: Nevertheless, the recurrent performance dip across datasets, when solely removing multiplicities, accentuates their integral role in PD representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-223: For instance, in the IMDB-B dataset, the performance disparity between the full MST and MST (w/o mult.) stands at \(3.62\%\), underscoring the multiplicities' influence on the efficacy of PD.
elements.sent-2411-224: To summarize, this ablation analysis emphasizes the criticality of the multiplicities bias within the MST framework.
elements.sent-2411-225: Their consistent and positive impact on the MST's performance across various datasets is a testament to their importance.
elements.sent-2411-226: The enhanced performance due to multiplicities underscores the MST's robustness, as evidenced not only through synthetic experiments but also in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-227: While the MST effectively meets our objectives by exhibiting strong performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets, there are certain limitations to our study that warrant attention.
elements.sent-2411-228: Firstly, the improved accuracies achieved through clustering warrant a deeper exploration.
elements.sent-2411-229: A thorough investigation is essential to determine the underlying mechanisms contributing to this improvement.
elements.sent-2411-230: Furthermore, it is widely acknowledged that the significance of a feature, specifically a point in the PD, is determined by its lifespan, which is schematically represented as the distance to the diagonal in Figure~\ref{fig:pds .}
elements.sent-2411-231: The greater the lifespan, the more significant the feature.
elements.sent-2411-232: In our experiments, we did not employ this assumption.
elements.sent-2411-233: However, we suggest that this property could be more comprehensively integrated into representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-234: Such integration may pave the way for the development of more refined and effective models in the future.
elements.sent-2411-235: In this paper, we present the Multiset Transformer, the first neural network based on attention mechanisms designed specifically for multisets as inputs, and it comes with rigorous theoretical guarantees of permutation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-236: This model leverages the multiplicities in a multiset by allocating more attention to elements with larger multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-237: The synthetic experiments demonstrate that our model achieves the intended design goals.
elements.sent-2411-238: In the domain of persistent diagram vectorization using neural networks, our approach surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods across most datasets.
elements.sent-2411-239: Furthermore, with its inherent ability to approximate sets into multisets via clustering, the Multiset Transformer offers a viable solution for datasets of any size, provided they can be efficiently clustered.. \bibliographystyle{tmlr .}

elements.sent-2411-260: To prove that \( A(Q, X) \) is permutation equivariant, we need to show that permuting the rows of \( Q \) and \( X \) in the same manner results in the same permutation of the rows of \( A(Q, X) \).
elements.sent-2411-261: Let \( P \) be an arbitrary permutation matrix.
elements.sent-2411-263: Since \( M \) can be seen as a function that operates on the multiset elements, i.e., rows of \(Q\) and \(X\), the order of the rows in \( PQ \) and \( PX \) will determine the order of the rows in \( M_{PQ \) and \( M_{PX \), giving: \begin{equation}M_{PQ} = PM_Q \quad and \quad M_{PX} = PM_X.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Substituting these into our equation: \begin{equation}A(PQ, PX) = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{PQX^{\top}P^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha\frac{(PM_Q-\mathbf 1)(PM_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(PM_Q-\mathbf 1)(PM_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) PX.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-265: Given that the softmax function is applied element-wise (or row-wise) and retains the order of elements, we can establish: \begin{equation}\operatorname{softmax}\left(P H P^{\top} \right) = P \operatorname{softmax}\left(H \right) P^{\top}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-266: Furthermore, permuting the rows or columns of a matrix only rearranges its elements without changing their values, so the sum of their squared magnitudes, and hence the Frobenius norm, remains unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-268: Substituting these into our equation, we have \begin{equation}A(PQ, PX) = \left(P\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)P^{\top}+\alpha \frac{P(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}P^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) PX = P \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha \frac{(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) P^{\top}PX = P \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha \frac{(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) X = PA(Q, X).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}A(PQ, PX) = \left(P\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)P^{\top}+\alpha \frac{P(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}P^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) PX = P \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha \frac{(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) P^{\top}PX = P \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)+\alpha \frac{(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|(M_Q-\mathbf 1)(M_X-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon} \right) X = PA(Q, X).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-269: Therefore, \( A(PQ, PX) = PA(Q, X) \), which proves that \( A(Q, X) \) is permutation equivariant.
elements.sent-2411-270: As seen in the proof to Theorem~\ref{thm:pe , we have \begin{equation}A_Q(PX) = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{Q(PX)^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + \frac{M_{\alpha}(M_{PX}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(M_{PX}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon}\right) PX = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}P^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + \frac{M_{\alpha}(PM_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(PM_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon}\right) PX = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}}\right)P^{\top} + \frac{M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}P^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon}\right) PX = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + \frac{M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon}\right)P^{\top} PX = \left(\operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QX^{\top}}{\sqrt{d}} \right) + \frac{M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}}{\|M_{\alpha}(M_{X}-\mathbf 1)^{\top}\|_F+\varepsilon}\right)X = A_Q(X)\end{equation} Therefore, \( A_Q(PX) = A_Q(X) \), which proves that \( A_Q(X) \) is permutation invariant.}
elements.subsection-2411-013: Ordinary Persistence and Extended Persistence in Graphs
elements.sent-2411-271: Given a graph \( G = (V, E) \), where \( V \) represents the vertices and \( E \) denotes the non-oriented edges.
elements.sent-2411-272: Given a function \( f : V \rightarrow \mathbb{R \) defined on the vertices of \( G \), we can construct sublevel graphs \( G_\alpha = (V_\alpha, E_\alpha) \) for each \( \alpha \in \mathbb{R \), such that \( V_\alpha = \{v \in V : f(v) \leq \alpha\} \) and \( E_\alpha = \{(v_1, v_2) \in E : v_1, v_2 \in V_\alpha\} \).}}
elements.sent-2411-273: As \( \alpha \) increases, we shall observe a sequence of these sublevel graphs, which is referred to as the filtration induced by \( f \).
elements.sent-2411-274: This filtration commences with an empty graph and culminates in the entirety of graph \( G \).
elements.sent-2411-275: A key aspect of persistence is its ability to track the emergence and dissolution of topological features, such as connected components and loops, throughout this filtration.
elements.sent-2411-276: For example, the birth time, denoted as \( \alpha_b \), marks the value at which a new connected component appears in \( G_{\alpha_b \).}
elements.sent-2411-277: This component will eventually amalgamate with another at a subsequent value \( \alpha_d \geq \alpha_b \), termed the death time.
elements.sent-2411-278: The lifespan of this component is captured by the interval \([\alpha_b, \alpha_d]\).
elements.sent-2411-279: In a similar vein, the birth and death times of loops in specific sublevel graphs are recorded.
elements.sent-2411-280: The aggregation of these intervals forms what is known as the barcode or ordinary PD of \( (G, f) \), which can be pictorially represented as a multiset in \(\mathbb{R ^2\).}
elements.sent-2411-281: Beyond the conventional scope of persistence, which primarily focuses on sublevel graphs, the concept of extended persistence introduces an additional dimension by considering superlevel graphs.
elements.sent-2411-282: Specifically, for a given \( \alpha \in \mathbb{R \), the superlevel graph \( G^\alpha = (V^\alpha, E^\alpha) \) is defined such that \( V^\alpha = \{v \in V : f(v) \geq \alpha\} \) and \( E^\alpha = \{(v_1, v_2) \in E : v_1, v_2 \in V^\alpha\} \).}
elements.sent-2411-283: As \( \alpha \) decreases, these superlevel graphs offer an alternative perspective, akin to viewing the same graph from a different direction when juxtaposed with sublevel graphs.
elements.sent-2411-284: This dual perspective ensures a more holistic capture of the topological intricacies inherent in a graph.
elements.sent-2411-285: Extended PDs, based on the juxtaposition of birth and death points, can be categorized into distinct types.
elements.sent-2411-286: As shown in the main paper, we represent these diagrams as multisets in \( \mathbb{R ^2 \), denoted as \( X_1, X_2, \ldots \), where the subscripted indices signify the different types of PDs.}
elements.sent-2411-287: While the above exposition offers a high-level introduction for clarity, readers seeking a deeper understanding are directed to seminal works in the field.
elements.sent-2411-288: For readers with an inclination towards the theoretical underpinnings of persistent homology, we further recommend \citet{edelsbrunner2022computational,oudot2017persistence for an in-depth exploration.}
elements.subsection-2411-014: Spectral Analysis with Heat Kernel Signature
elements.sent-2411-289: As alluded to in Subsection~\ref{subsec:features , we employ the Heat Kernel Signature (HKS) as filtrations to derive the extended PDs used in our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-290: For the sake of completeness, we provide a brief introduction to HKS here.
elements.sent-2411-291: For an in-depth exploration, we direct readers to seminal works by \citet{carriere2020perslay , \citet{sun2009concise , and \citet{hu2014stable .}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: HKS is a spectral descriptor, originating from the spectral decomposition of graph Laplacians, and has proven to be a powerful tool for graph analysis.
elements.sent-2411-293: Consider a graph \( G \) with its vertex set represented as \( V = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\} \).
elements.sent-2411-294: The adjacency matrix of this graph is denoted by \( A \).
elements.sent-2411-295: The degree matrix \( D \) is a diagonal matrix where each entry \( D_{i,i \) is the sum of the \(i\)-th row of \( A \).}
elements.sent-2411-296: The normalized graph Laplacian, \( L_n \), is given by the equation \begin{equation}L_n = I - D^{-\frac{1}{2}} A D^{-\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} where \( I \) stands for the identity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-297: This Laplacian possesses an orthonormal basis of eigenfunctions, denoted as \( \Psi = \{\psi_1, \ldots, \psi_n\} \), and the associated eigenvalues adhere to the inequality \( 0 \leq \lambda_1 \leq \ldots \leq \lambda_n \leq 2 \).
elements.sent-2411-298: The HKS, parameterized by \( t \), is defined as the function \( \mathrm{hks _{t \) on the vertices of \( G \) by the relation: \begin{equation}\mathrm{hks}_{t} : v \mapsto \sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(-t\lambda_k) \psi_k(v)^2\end{equation} It is noteworthy that \(\mathrm{hks _{t}\) maps vertices to the real line \(\mathbb R\), thereby inducing a natural filtration for the graph.}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: The parameter \(t\) serves as a hyperparameter, the specifics of which are provided in Table~\ref{tab:hyp .}
elements.sent-2411-300: The spectral features are a synthesis of the eigenvalues of the normalized graph Laplacian and the deciles of the HKS.
elements.sent-2411-301: Importantly, these features are consistent with those used in the experiment by \citet{carriere2020perslay .}
elements.sent-2411-302: This section includes omited details on the MST.
elements.sent-2411-303: The multihead attention mechanism, as proposed in \citep{vaswani2017attention , is also a pivotal component of Transformer.}
elements.sent-2411-304: The essence of multihead attention lies in its ability to allow different heads to focus on diverse segments of the input data.
elements.sent-2411-305: This is achieved by linearly projecting the \( Q, K, V \) vectors into dimensions \(d_i,d_i,p_i\) respectively, as outlined by \citep{vaswani2017attention, zhang2023dive .}
elements.sent-2411-306: An added advantage of this projection is the relaxation of the constraint that necessitated \( Q \) and \( K \) to possess identical dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-307: To formalize the operation of the multihead attention mechanism, consider the \(i\)-th head out of a total of \(h\) heads.
elements.sent-2411-308: The output for this head is computed as: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) :=\operatorname{Concat}\left(\operatorname{h}_1, \ldots, \operatorname{h}_{h}\right) W^O, \operatorname{h}_i :=\operatorname{Att}\left(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V\right) .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) :=\operatorname{Concat}\left(\operatorname{h}_1, \ldots, \operatorname{h}_{h}\right) W^O, \operatorname{h}_i :=\operatorname{Att}\left(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V\right) .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-309: In the above equations, the projections are characterized by parameter matrices \(W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R ^{d_q \times d_i}\), \(W_i^K \in \mathbb{R ^{d_k \times d_i}\), \(W_i^V \in \mathbb{R ^{d_v \times p_i}\), and \(W^O \in \mathbb{R ^{(\Sigma p_i) \times q}\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: This multihead attention mechanism ensures that the model can capture a richer set of relationships and dependencies in the data by allowing each head to focus on different aspects of the input.
elements.sent-2411-311: For the sake of clarity, the discussions in the paper focuses on the single head attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-312: However, it's worth noting that the multihead version can be readily inferred from our descriptions, leveraging the principles delineated in the preceding section.
elements.sent-2411-313: In orchestrating with layer normalization (LN), multiple versions of the multiset attention block (MAB) can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-314: The main paper presents the following formulation: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MAB}(Q, X) := \operatorname{LN}(H + \operatorname{FFN}(H)), H := \operatorname{LN}(Q + A(Q,X)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-315: In an alternative formulation, we have: \begin{equation}\operatorname{MAB}(Q, X) := H + \operatorname{FFN}(\operatorname{LN}(H)), H := Q + A\left(\operatorname{LN}(Q), \operatorname{LN}(X)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-316: The former is denominated as the post-LN MAB while the latter is termed the pre-LN MAB.
elements.sent-2411-317: A salient observation is that the application of \(\operatorname{LN}\) retains the multiplicities \(M_X\) in \(\operatorname{LN (X)\).}
elements.sent-2411-318: According to the study by \citet{xiong2020layer , the Pre-LN Transformers exhibit superior stability relative to the post-LN variants.}
elements.sent-2411-319: For the sake of clarity and to ensure consistency in the ensuing discussions, we will denote both formulations under the generic term \(\operatorname{MAB}\) due to their functional similarities within the overarching framework.
elements.subsection-2411-017: Motivation of Multiset Transformer with Clustering Preprocessing
elements.sent-2411-320: The primary motivation behind incorporating clustering preprocessing in the MST framework is to balance scalability with the preservation of data details.
elements.sent-2411-321: A critical insight from the complexity analysis presented in the main paper is the direct correlation between the size of the MST model and the multiset size \(n\).
elements.sent-2411-322: Recognizing that neighboring elements in a multiset often hold similar information, clustering these elements before deploying the MST emerges as a strategic move.
elements.sent-2411-323: This preprocessing step stands particularly significant for MST, more so than for the Set Transformer, due to its unique handling of data points.
elements.sent-2411-324: In MST, the information inherent in the elements is not simply discarded; rather, it undergoes a transformation where it is incorporated into the multiplicities of a representative point.
elements.sent-2411-325: This nuanced approach ensures that crucial data characteristics are retained, albeit in an aggregated form.
elements.sent-2411-326: Consequently, we adopt a preprocessing strategy where multisets are clustered prior to their engagement with the MST.
elements.sent-2411-327: This methodology not only maintains comparable performance levels but also leads to a significant reduction in computational complexity, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the model.
